summary: Cantrill recounts presenting DTrace at USENIX '04 and being asked by an IBM employee when it would be ported to Linux. He argues against the prevailing view that operating systems are commodities, asserting instead that the OS is a 'nexus of innovation.' He maintains that Solaris 10's technologies like DTrace, Zones, and ZFS demonstrate this innovation, and users who want these features should run Solaris rather than expect ports to Linux.
key_points: DTrace paper presentation at USENIX '04 was well-received
IBM employee asked when DTrace would be ported to Linux
Many believe operating systems are commodities - all basically the same
Sun disagrees: the operating system is a nexus of innovation
Solaris 10 technologies (DTrace, Zones, ZFS) prove OS innovation is possible
If you want the innovation, run Solaris - don't expect Linux ports
money_quotes: We believe in choice; we believe that people should pick the best operating system for the task at hand.
Many seem to believe that the operating system is a 'commodity' – that all operating systems are basically the same. We disagree: we believe that the operating system is a nexus of innovation.
Please stop asking if we're going to port these features to Linux – if you want to take advantage of our OS innovation, run Solaris.
key_insight: The operating system is not a commodity but a nexus of innovation, and companies that invest in OS-level innovation should use that differentiation as a competitive advantage rather than giving it away to competing platforms.
